metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = allowed/quantized orbits/nonzero area; CRIMSON = classical prediction/zero zero-point energy
metadata.note: EXCLUSIONS: no Airy-function connection formulas, no action-integral evaluation, no Langer correction
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Draw the motion of a harmonic oscillator in position-momentum space. As it swings back and forth, it traces an ellipse. Classically, the ellipse can be any size — even infinitely small, with the particle sitting perfectly at rest.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: This is Liam, in for Bear. Draw the motion of a harmonic oscillator in position-momentum space. As it swings back and forth, it traces an ellipse. Classically,

beats.narration_text: Quantum mechanics says no. The allowed ellipses must enclose areas that come in fixed chunks — one-half, one and a half, two and a half times Planck's constant. The smallest allowed ellipse still encloses a nonzero area. The particle cannot be still.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Quantum mechanics says no. The allowed ellipses must enclose areas that come in fixed chunks — one-half, one and a half, two and a half times Planck's constant.

beats.narration_text: Classically, a particle can sit at zero energy with zero motion. Quantum mechanics forbids it — the ground state has nonzero energy even at absolute zero. Why must the smallest orbit still enclose a finite patch of phase space?

props.headline: Classically, a particle can sit at zero energy with zero motion.…

card.headline: Smallest orbit: area = h/2, not zero. Why?
card.dek: Why must the ground-state orbit still enclose a finite patch of phase space?

beats.narration_text: The classical rule for allowed orbits came from Bohr and Sommerfeld: the action integral around one complete orbit — momentum times position, integrated around the loop — must equal an integer times Planck's constant. One orbit per integer.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The classical rule for allowed orbits came from Bohr and Sommerfeld: the action integral around one complete orbit — momentum times position, integrated around

beats.narration_text: That rule gave integer multiples of h. Integer zero: rest. Integer one: first orbit. Fine for high quantum numbers. But the actual quantum-mechanical spectrum is n plus one-half times h-bar times omega — not n times. The half is missing.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: That rule gave integer multiples of h. Integer zero: rest. Integer one: first orbit. Fine for high quantum numbers. But the actual quantum-mechanical spectrum i

beats.narration_text: For the ground state, n equals zero, so the classical rule predicts zero energy. The harmonic oscillator would sit at the bottom. But it doesn't — it has energy h-bar omega over two. The one-half matters enormously for the lowest state.

props.headline: For the ground state, n equals zero, so the classical rule…

build.suggested: suggested prompt: For the ground state, n equals zero, so the classical rule predicts zero energy. The harmonic oscillator would sit at the bottom. But it doesn't — it has energy

beats.narration_text: The fix comes from wave mechanics. At each classical turning point — where the particle momentarily stops — the wave function must match smoothly to an exponentially decaying tail in the forbidden region. That matching picks up an extra quarter-turn of phase, called the Maslov index.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The fix comes from wave mechanics. At each classical turning point — where the particle momentarily stops — the wave function must match smoothly to an exponent

beats.narration_text: A bound state has two turning points — left and right. Each contributes a quarter turn. Together they add a half-turn — half of Planck's constant — to the required action. The quantization condition becomes n plus one-half times h, not n times h.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: A bound state has two turning points — left and right. Each contributes a quarter turn. Together they add a half-turn — half of Planck's constant — to the requi

beats.narration_text: For the ground state, that half is the entire thing. The orbit area must equal h over two — not zero. You cannot squeeze the ellipse to a point. The zero-point energy is that leftover half-unit of phase-space area, made mandatory by the wave-matching condition at both turning points.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: For the ground state, that half is the entire thing. The orbit area must equal h over two — not zero. You cannot squeeze the ellipse to a point. The zero-point

beats.narration_text: Each turning point adds a quarter turn. Two turning points add a half. Orbit area equals n plus one-half times h. At n equals zero: area equals h over two.

props.headline: Each turning point adds a quarter turn. Two turning points add…

card.headline: ∮ p dx = (n + ½)h  →  zero-point area = h/2
card.dek: Two Maslov quarter-turns at the turning points add up to the half that saves zero-point energy.

beats.narration_text: This isn't just a correction. Without the half, the ground state has zero energy — the particle can sit still. With the half, it cannot. The half enforces the Heisenberg uncertainty principle: squeezing both position and momentum to zero is forbidden, and the minimum orbit area is the minimum uncertainty product.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: This isn't just a correction. Without the half, the ground state has zero energy — the particle can sit still. With the half, it cannot. The half enforces the H

beats.narration_text: Zero-point energy is real: liquid helium never freezes at atmospheric pressure because its quantum zero-point motion is strong enough to prevent crystallization even at absolute zero. Every atom in a crystal vibrates with this minimum energy. It shifts the Casimir force between metal plates. It is not a convention — it has measurable consequences.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Zero-point energy is real: liquid helium never freezes at atmospheric pressure because its quantum zero-point motion is strong enough to prevent crystallization

beats.narration_text: Illustrative: for a harmonic oscillator with angular frequency omega equals 10-to-the-14 radians per second — roughly a near-infrared molecular vibration — the zero-point energy h-bar omega over two equals about 33 milli-electron-volts. That is the energy the oscillator has at absolute zero. The classical prediction: zero. The measured zero-point shift in molecular spectra: tens of millielectron-volts. The half makes all the difference.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Illustrative: for a harmonic oscillator with angular frequency omega equals 10-to-the-14 radians per second — roughly a near-infrared molecular vibration — the

beats.narration_text: The question: why must the smallest orbit in phase space enclose a finite patch, not a point? Because at each classical turning point, wave-matching picks up a quarter-turn of phase. Two turning points give a half — and that half forces the minimum orbit area to h over two, not zero. The zero-point energy is that mandatory half-unit of action. Quantum mechanics.

props.headline: The question: why must the smallest orbit in phase space enclose…

card.headline: Two Maslov phases → orbit area ≥ h/2 → zero-point energy.
card.dek: The wave-matching condition at each turning point adds a quarter-turn that forbids the ground state from resting.
